title: 978 Days
subtitle:
I was scheduled for brain surgery on October 23rd 2023.
While I waited, something happened.
It happened on October 7th in Israel.
shareDescription:
I was scheduled for brain surgery on October 23rd 2023.
While I waited, something happened.
It happened on October 7th in Israel.

author.name: Kevin Gwin
author.bio: Peace Activist and member of the Greater Dayton Peace Coalition

content:
I will open by saying that my positions on the Middle East were arrived at organically. I have no ties to the Middle East, or anywhere else for that matter. I am a mongrel. I have mostly European blood, with a dab of Native American for flavor. I have never felt any sort of ancestral loyalty to any race, nationality, or otherwise. I was raised to believe that all people were equal, and should be treated as equals. And I personally believe that we are all connected. Not just metaphorically, but physically. We are intrinsically connected to every living thing on Earth, as well as the entire Universe.

For me, 2023 started fine. I had made up my mind to start trying to paint again. I had spent some time and effort at it decades earlier, but I had too much on my plate, and had to let it go. But by 2023, I had been retired for a few years. Busy, but retired. So I had the time, I just needed to put some effort into it and see if it at least made me happy, even if I wasn't much good at it.

Not long after I began experimenting, I realized that my vision was changing, and it wasn't good. I kept at it, kept trying, but my vision was getting worse. So I made an appointment with an optometrist. After what I thought was a thorough eye exam, the optometrist told me my eyes were fine. I just needed a new eyeglass prescription. So, I purchased new glasses. New glasses that really did me little good. They were bi-focal, and the improvement in my distance vision was negligible at best, and my close vision was really no different at all. I had been wearing reading glasses for a long time already, but they were no help with the vision issues I was experiencing.

I reluctantly had to give up on painting. It was a disappointment, but I was not unfamiliar with disappointment, a day without some sort of disappointment was unusual, so I just pressed on with my life. I had other things to keep me occupied.

By summer, my vision had gotten much worse. I was having difficulty reading. I had already seen an eye doctor, so I tried to just power through the difficulty for as long as I could. Then it got to the point that when I was trying to read a page of text, it was as if it had been ripped in half vertically, and the two halves were shoved together, leaving the center of the page just gone.

At that point, it was intolerable. I bypassed the strip-mall optometrist and made an appointment with an eye surgeon.

When I arrived at my appointment, before I saw the doctor, I was put through a series of tests, which took about 30-45 minutes. I was then sent to an exam room to wait. When the doctor arrived, he looked over the tests, asked me about 5 questions, and told me I had a pituitary tumor.

I finally had a real diagnosis!

An MRI confirmed the tumor and also explained a cluster of other health issues that had gone undiagnosed for years. The endocrinologist I was referred to didn't hesitate - the tumor had grown large enough to press against my optic nerves. Left untreated, that kind of pressure can eventually result in permanent blindness. And I may have had the tumor for decades at that point. He sent me to Ohio State with a referral to whom he called "the best he had ever seen," a pioneer in the type of surgery I needed. He was concerned, and told me it couldn't wait.

This was in September of 2023. After consultation, and another MRI, I was scheduled for brain surgery on October 23rd 2023.

While I waited, something happened.

It happened on October 7th in Israel.

The story coming from mainstream news media and Israel was a tale of a surprise attack by Hamas against Israel. Details were scarce at first, but as hours and days passed, I noticed sharp contradictions between different sources. The narratives being repeated by Israeli spokespersons and mainstream media alike began to seem to me not only incomplete, but fabricated - a steady stream of tall tales, including horrifying atrocities. All clearly intended to dehumanize not only Hamas, but Palestinians as a people.

From Israel, I was hearing horrific tales of beheaded babies, babies put in ovens, rapes and other atrocities, and these stories were being repeated by government officials, mainstream press, and even PBS.

But none of that even made sense.

I was also seeing eyewitness accounts from young Israelis describing barely escaping with their lives when the cars they were fleeing the Nova Music Festival in were targeted by IDF Apache helicopters. I saw photos of cars and homes so destroyed that it could only have been done by high powered rockets or shells fired from above.

Until then, I had considered myself well-informed - I had watched and read news almost exclusively for decades. What I was now seeing changed me, my world view, and my trust for mainstream media, permanently.

Prior to October 7th, I was as ignorant, and uninformed as most Americans when it came to the plight of the indigenous people of Palestine. Like most Americans, I didn't give it much thought. These were distant conflicts on the other side of the world, I told myself. They had nothing to do with me.

I am now deeply ashamed of my own ignorance and indifference.

We are all connected! Everything is connected! And everyone matters!

It was time to learn more. I started where I felt I could find solid footing: I sought out Jewish voices - writers, historians, activists - who were publicly expressing their own anger and grief over what was unfolding. That search opened a trove of history and testimony I had never encountered, despite decades of following the news closely.

On mainstream news, many were asking why Israel, who had kept the Palestinians in Gaza behind a prison wall, unlike any other in existence, could have possibly been taken by surprise.

Gazans lived behind a heavily fortified, steel and concrete wall, 20 feet above ground topped with surveillance cameras and remote controlled weapons, a deep subterranean wall equipped with sensors, and a buffer zone outside the wall with motion sensors, barbed wire, and killer robots.

None of this made any sense. It was truly impossible to believe.

While I continued waiting for my scheduled surgery, I kept reading, and learning. In addition to the Jewish voices that I had sought out for clarity, I was searching for, and discovering many independent news sources and journalists who were speaking the unvarnished truth, as well as providing historical context to everything I was witnessing in absolute horror.

In spite of struggling to even read, I learned a lot.

And when I began responding publicly to what I was learning, and witnessing with my own eyes, I was dismissed as a conspiracy theorist. Old friends unfriended me. Facebook removed posts. I found myself out on a limb, largely alone - with a handful of local friends who shared my alarm, and many others who either stayed silent or voiced support for Israel.

But this had become deeply personal to me. As a man who had suffered the horrific loss of a child myself, watching the deliberate slaughter of innocent people, mostly women and children was not only heartbreaking, but kept me moving between tears and rage hour after hour. I began to see every lost child as a personal loss.

It was my solemn duty to speak out. I no longer cared whether it cost me friends or family. It was my duty.

I naively believed, truly believed, that when enough people heard the truth about what was happening, and what lead up to it, (That Israel had been attacking the Gaza strip the entire year before October 7th, and truly throughout the existence of Israel.) I believed that the people of this world would rise in solidarity with the oppressed, and downtrodden people of Palestine, and force the US to stop helping Israel slaughter innocent people. I was wrong.

But I never gave up, and I never will. I can't.

I was still waiting for brain surgery. As I waited, I watched, and I learned.

Within two days of the 7th, I had already seen enough to believe that this was not some sort of intelligence failure. It was not a blunder of the Israeli Defense Forces. They were not "caught off guard." The failures were deliberate. Israel, and it seemed all of the Western press, were lying to defend the indefensible.

I was still somewhat guarded in my public posts, but was still seen by many as a terrorist sympathizer, and conspiracy theorist. But in spite of being almost blind at the time, I could not remain silent. And even though I was largely ignored, I kept speaking out. I could not divert my near blind eyes from the terrifying outsized response of Israel in the name of "self-defense."

At this point, many of the posts I was making in my horror and grief were met with comments trying to shift all of the blame to Hamas. And many are still doing that. So I was careful in my early posts to refrain from giving anyone the impression that I supported Hamas, even though I have come to understand that Hamas was always inevitable, because resistance is always inevitable under occupation. And because there is no member of the Hamas resistance that wasn't born in a prison.

I did not remain out on that limb, seemingly alone, for very long. I realized that as disappointed as I was in the many friends I have, I could not force them with the truth, with guilt, or with shame, to rise and stand on the right side of history. Or to speak out.

And honestly, I had no idea whether they even believed what I was sharing with them. But if they didn't, they could see how important it was to me, so if they were really my friends, it was their responsibility to make an effort to understand.

Innocent people were being slaughtered. I needed allies. I needed other people who got it.

So I began reaching out, and ultimately, I made friends from all over the world who shared my ongoing heartache, grief, and distress. They became my allies, my sounding boards, and many became my dear friends.

Meanwhile, this brutal slaughter was being referred to and characterized, as a war. It still is to this day, even though it does not bear any resemblance to a war. The continuous bombing, fire bombing, robot murder drones, sniper killings, the use of white phosphorus, all on innocent civilians, mostly women and children, are not acts of war, they are war crimes.

October 23rd came and went. I had surgery, and was back at home in a couple of days. I had some recovery time ahead of me, but I could already tell that my vision was beginning to improve. My surgeon told me that they were able to remove the entire tumor. But because this damaged my pituitary gland, which is responsible for regulating your entire endocrine system, I would likely be on hormone replacement therapy for the rest of my life. But in the days following my surgery, I already felt better than I had in years...physically.

While my vision was improving slowly, I didn't need perfect vision to gain clarity on what was happening in Gaza, nor my own country's role in it. It was a bloodbath. It was genocide.

That much was crystal clear to me. I was still struggling though. Wondering when my government that has seemingly prided itself on being a model of human rights, democracy, and freedom, would do anything at all to stop this.

I don't remember exactly when it was. But it finally became clear to me that my government was not only supplying arms, and funding the most live streamed, in your face, genocide in history, but was a full partner in it.

And they were never going to do anything to stop it. That moment of clarity came long before the 2024 presidential election, so it has been a full partnership through two administrations. And during all of this time, every resolution for a ceasefire that came to a vote in the United Nations, was vetoed by the United States. My country was going out of its way to support a genocide

I have spent almost every day of the last two and a half years since, writing politicians, speaking out on social media, engaging in protests, signing petitions, and breaking as a human being.

I do not want to spend most of my free time fighting for justice, fighting oppression, fighting against a genocidal terrorist regime that has clearly dreamt of doing exactly what they are doing now, throughout their existence.

But I have to. Everyone has to.

In the months that followed, a clearer picture emerged.

Israel was aware of the planned incursion into occupied Palestinian territory by Hamas for a long time. One report claimed that they knew it was coming as far back as 2018.

The commander of the area where the Nova Music festival was being held was warned that "something was going to happen," and tried to call it off. But that request was denied.

The original site for the Nova Music Festival was much further away from Gaza, but was changed at the last minute to a site within walking distance. So much of what happened on that fateful day cannot simply be coincidence.

A stand down order was issued to the skeleton crew of troops in the area in the early morning hours of October 7th. 

And somehow, one of the most sophisticated surveillance systems in the world was nonfunctional on October 7th. The story about why has evolved, but with that much surveillance and remote controlled weaponry, it is hard to believe that Hamas was able to take their entire surveillance system out. (But there really has been nothing but lies from the Israeli government and IDF spokespersons)

Israel did not mount any kind of response, or defense to the incursion for hours. And when they did respond, 28 Apache helicopters fired 28,000 rounds into the Nova Music Festival and area Kibbutzim, reloaded, and did it again. The majority of Israeli casualties on October 7th, were victims of IDF shelling.

Israel began bombing civilians in Gaza on October 7th. In spite of the fabrication that Israel was doing their best to limit civilian casualties, they were in fact, targeting civilians throughout. Including Snipers shooting infants in the head and chest, sending killer drones playing the sound of crying babies to pull concerned civilians into the street to be killed. (I saw the same quad copter drones chasing and killing children in the streets.) Firebombing refugee tents, and dropping white phosphorus on children. There is so much more, it will fill books that have yet to be written.

There is the story of Hind Rajab. A 5 yr. old little girl trapped in a car with dead family members for hours. Begging for help on a telephone, while the IDF fired over 300 rounds into the small car she was in.

This is not an act of war, it is a crime against humanity.

We now have firsthand accounts of the systematic rape of Palestinians in Israeli custody - documented by Al Jazeera in their investigative film Bodies of Evidence: Israel's Darkest Weapon, and corroborated by the International Criminal Court, the United Nations, and UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese. Among the testimonies collected is that of Muhammad al-Bakri, a Gaza civil servant who describes in precise detail being stripped, blindfolded, handcuffed, and sexually assaulted by Israeli soldiers using trained dogs, while soldiers laughed and filmed. He was not alone. Multiple former prisoners gave similar accounts. Rights organizations including the Palestinian Centre for Human Rights and Euro-Med Human Rights Monitor have documented the same pattern.

Israel has been murdering, raping, robbing, maiming, and humiliating the Palestinian people for over 78 years - and every year it gets worse.

There has been nothing in my life that has broken my spirit, short of losing my own child, as much as these last 978 days as of June 11, 2026.

I have felt the pain and hopelessness of these people in my soul. I have felt utterly devastated by what I have seen with my own eyes.

The futility of begging my own government to please stop arming this terrorist genocide has not stopped me from continuing to appeal to their own sense of morality.

But morality is in short supply in the power structures of the imperialist predators that seem to be ruling the world we are living in.

The conclusion that many arrived at early on, including myself, is that Israel wanted October 7th more so than Hamas. They needed an October 7th to provide pretext for genocide.

That is why there were so many lies created about the events of that day. In reality, Hamas had a mission.

Their mission was to capture prisoners to be used in exchange for their own people who were being held hostage and tortured in Israeli prisons.

It would only make their intended goal much more impossible to have engaged in ANY of the atrocities that Israel accused them of.

But, what many have concluded from the many accusations Israel has made against Hamas, is that they have become less accusations, and more admissions of guilt.

Because everything they accused Hamas of, they have done themselves. On a much larger scale.

The true number of Palestinian casualties of this genocide will likely never be known.

And throughout the last 2 years, the official death toll has been hovering at around 70,000 and that, is simply impossible.

More realistic estimates are between 500,000 and 750,000.

Since October 7th 2023, Israel's reign of terror has included a number of other countries.

Lebanon, next to the occupied territories of Palestine has been the harshest victim. With no concern for civilians, children, or ancient historical sites. Israel has been relentless in their brutality.

Tens of thousands of attacks, thousands killed, and many thousands of injuries. Israel has also attacked Syria and Yemen.

This is Colonial expansionism, and every bit of it has been supported and financed by my own country. Even while the overwhelming majority of American citizens are against all of it.

Israel even manipulated a compliant American president into war with Iran on the basis of a false claim they have been making for forty years.

In February of this year, a full-scale war with Iran has launched. And among other things, my country bombed a school on the first day, killing over 165 school children

There is more, so much more, and I do my best to shout it from the rooftops every day.

Have I changed any minds? I have. I know of several at least that have given me credit for that.

But there are many more that have shifted their beliefs from the propagandized, brainwashed, people who just trust whatever the propaganda machines tell them, to people who can at least accept the truth, even as they remain mostly silent.

After what I have witnessed, and experienced over the last several years, I have never been more ashamed of my own country, or the entire western world.

War crimes, crimes against humanity, international crimes. All being ignored by what I once believed to be a civilized world.

title: Occupation Does Not Erase Resistance: Lebanon, Iran, and the Illusion of Durable Control
subtitle: A reflection on Lebanon, Iran, and the recurring failure of military occupation to produce durable control, arguing that lasting security requires political legitimacy, reciprocity, and accountability.
shareDescription: A reflection on Lebanon, Iran, and the recurring failure of military occupation to produce durable control, arguing that lasting security requires political legitimacy, reciprocity, and accountability.

author.name: Youssef A. Elzein
author.bio: Youssef A. Elzein is a Lebanese-born civil engineer, longtime Dayton resident, and community advocate whose work focuses on public service, immigrant inclusion, equity, and building stronger communities.

content:
A Note Before You Read

I am not a political scientist or military analyst. I hold no credentials that confer authority on these questions. What I do bring is a commitment to reading carefully, researching honestly, and following evidence where it leads, even when it is inconvenient.

I write from curiosity and conscience, not from ideology. I write to expose inhumane practices while challenging a media environment in which only one side of the story is often centered in Western and US coverage. I ask that you engage this piece on the strength of its arguments and the documented record it draws from.

I

Memorial Day and the Cost of Repetition

Memorial Day passed just days ago, and with it came the familiar ceremonies: flags, speeches, the solemn roll call of the fallen. We honored, as we should, the men and women who gave their lives in service to this country. But honoring sacrifice honestly also means asking what that sacrifice was for - and whether it achieved what we were told it would.

II

The Recent Record

Consider the recent record. In Iraq, the United States toppled a government, dismantled an army, and spent two decades and nearly 4,500 American lives attempting to build a stable, democratic order. What emerged instead was a sectarian state with deep Iranian influence, an ISIS insurgency born directly from the betrayal and abandonment of the Sunni community by both the US and Iran - Washington breaking its pledges to the 100,000 armed Sunni fighters who had stood with American forces during the surge, while allowing Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki to systematically exclude and persecute Sunni political leaders - and a population that regards the intervention with ambivalence at best and fury at worst.

In Afghanistan, the United States waged the longest war in its history - twenty years, over 2,400 service members killed, hundreds of thousands of Afghan lives lost - only to watch the Taliban retake Kabul in eleven days once the withdrawal came.

In Syria, American forces were drawn into a multi-sided conflict with no clear mandate, no exit strategy, and no enduring outcome that could justify the cost in lives and treasure.

III

Iran in Two Acts

And then Iran - in two acts.

In June 2025, Operation Midnight Hammer struck Iranian nuclear facilities with B-2 bombers. The administration declared victory. The crisis was not resolved.

Then came February 2026, when the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury - assassinating Supreme Leader Khamenei and triggering Iranian retaliatory strikes across all six Gulf states, targeting airports, oil refineries, and US military bases throughout the region. The Strait of Hormuz was closed. Global oil markets convulsed. American service members across the Gulf were placed directly in the line of fire - not because America was attacked, but because Washington chose to enter someone else's war.

Only 21 percent of Americans supported the February strikes. The people who bear the risk were not consulted. They rarely are.

What makes this more troubling is what was known and ignored beforehand. The US Joint Chiefs of Staff explicitly warned President Trump that strikes on Iran would likely prompt the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. He dismissed the warning.

As for America's Gulf allies - Saudi Arabia and the UAE had publicly signaled their neutrality and that their territory would not be used to launch attacks on Iran, a signal intended to insulate them from Iranian retaliation. Whatever private consultations may have occurred at the elite level, Gulf populations woke on March 1 to Iranian missiles striking their airports, ports, and energy infrastructure without warning or preparation. The restraint those governments had carefully performed bought them no protection. Their people paid the price for a decision that was not theirs to make.

IV

Syria and American Diplomatic Incoherence

Syria deserves its own section, because it produced the most extraordinary illustration of American diplomatic incoherence in modern history - and the most direct rebuke to one of Washington's most loudly proclaimed principles.

The United States and Israel have long insisted, as a matter of solemn policy, that they do not negotiate with terrorists. It is a position stated with particular force whenever Palestinian, Lebanese, or Iranian interlocutors are involved.

Hold that principle in mind while considering the following documented facts. Ahmed al-Sharaa - then known by his nom de guerre Abu Mohammad al-Jolani - was arrested by American troops in Iraq for membership in al-Qaeda. He later led Hayat Tahrir al-Sham, which the United States formally designated a foreign terrorist organization. The US placed a $10 million bounty on his head.

In December 2024, his forces swept across Syria and toppled Bashar al-Assad. The Biden administration's Assistant Secretary of State then flew to Damascus, met with al-Sharaa, and lifted the bounty - explaining that "if we are having a discussion, it is incoherent to have a bounty on his head."

By January 2025, al-Sharaa was named Syria's interim president. By November 2025, he was welcomed to the White House by Donald Trump, who sent him a cordial handwritten note hailing him as a future "great leader." It was the first time a Syrian president had ever been received at the White House. The man with the American terrorist designation and the $10 million bounty is now Washington's partner in the fight against ISIS.

The United States and Israel do not negotiate with terrorists - unless, it appears, the negotiation serves a purpose they find convenient. That qualifier renders the principle meaningless, and the people of Lebanon, Palestine, and Iran are entitled to say so.

V

The Pattern

In each case - Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria, Iran - the pattern is the same: military dominance could not substitute for political legitimacy. Regime change could not manufacture consent. And resistance - however brutally suppressed - did not stay suppressed.

I write this not to diminish the valor of those who served. I write it because their sacrifice deserves better than repetition. As I watch the current campaign unfolding in Lebanon, with the same logic, the same language, and the same confident predictions of a durable military solution, I find myself returning to a question that no one in power seems willing to answer honestly: when has this ever worked in the Middle East?

There is a question that rarely gets asked in the breathless coverage of Israel's expanding military campaign in Lebanon: even if the stated objective - dismantling Hezbollah - were fully achieved, what prevents another resistance movement from emerging in its place?

History offers a clear answer. It always does.

That question deserves to sit at the center of any honest analysis of what is unfolding today - not just in Lebanon, but in the broader diplomatic theater surrounding the stalled US-Iran ceasefire and the land being swallowed in southern Lebanon while the world watches.

VI

The Geometry of the Delay

When the United States and Iran announced a two-week ceasefire in early April 2026, the ink had barely dried before Israeli forces launched a major military operation across Lebanon - killing hundreds within hours of the announcement. Iran's Foreign Minister put it plainly: "The US must choose - ceasefire or continued war via Israel. It cannot have both."

That was not rhetoric. It was a structural observation.

The ceasefire's terms were, from the outset, deliberately ambiguous on the question of Lebanon. Israel insisted Lebanon was excluded, the Lebanese government obliged, while Iran insisted it was included. The US, rather than resolving this ambiguity, allowed it to persist - effectively granting Israel a window in which to operate without diplomatic constraint, even as a nominal truce was in effect.

Since then, Israeli forces have crossed the Litani River - the deepest incursion into Lebanon since 1982 - and now occupy approximately one-fifth of Lebanese territory. Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz has openly declared plans to hold a "security zone" up to the Litani River indefinitely. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich has suggested redrawn northern borders along the Litani altogether. These are not military commanders speaking off-script. These are cabinet ministers stating policy.

The connection between the ceasefire delay and the land being seized is not speculative. It is sequential, documented, and - to anyone watching carefully - entirely legible. A final agreement that locks in a ceasefire also constrains further territorial consolidation. The delay is the strategy.

VII

The Litani Is Not a New Ambition

It is worth noting that Israel's first invasion of Lebanon in 1978 was named, without irony, Operation Litani. Military campaigns are rarely named arbitrarily. The river has been a strategic objective for decades - valued not only for what it denies armed movements, but for what controlling it confers: water resources, territorial depth, and a buffer that, if held long enough, becomes a fait accompli.

Israel maintained an occupation of southern Lebanon from 1982 until the year 2000 - eighteen years - under nearly identical justifications to those offered today. That occupation ended not through a negotiated disarmament of Lebanese resistance, but through sustained guerrilla pressure that made the cost of staying untenable. The force that drove Israel out in 2000 was Hezbollah.

But here is what is rarely acknowledged: Hezbollah was not the beginning of Lebanese resistance. It was the latest iteration of it.

VIII

Resistance Before Hezbollah

Armed resistance in southern Lebanon predates Hezbollah by decades. The Palestine Liberation Organization operated from Lebanese territory against Israel from 1968 onward. The Amal movement - founded in 1973 as the political voice of Lebanon's dispossessed Shia community - formed its military wing in 1975, and saw its ranks swell to 14,000 fighters after Israel's 1978 invasion displaced 300,000 Shia from the south.

When Israel invaded in 1982 and occupied Beirut, the Lebanese National Resistance Front was born within weeks - a broad coalition of Lebanese factions that carried out over 1,000 operations against Israeli forces in its first two years alone, turning southern Lebanon into what one contemporary account described as "a quagmire for the most powerful armed forces in the Middle East."

Hezbollah itself emerged from the wreckage of that invasion, trained and supported by Iran, but rooted in the same Lebanese Shia population that had already been resisting occupation for years under Amal and the National Resistance Front.

This is the central irony that policymakers consistently decline to reckon with: occupation, in Lebanon's modern history, has not suppressed armed resistance. It has generated it - in successive waves, each one more organized and more capable than the last.

IX

The Question That Goes Unasked

Analysts and officials debate at length whether Hezbollah can be militarily defeated, degraded, or dismantled. Far fewer ask the follow-on question: and then what?

Lebanon is a country that has survived a civil war, multiple Israeli invasions, Syrian occupation, political assassination, economic collapse, and a port explosion that leveled a significant area of its capital city. Its population is not passive. Its geography - mountainous, fractured, difficult to hold - has confounded occupying armies for centuries.

The notion that a military campaign sufficient to dismantle one organized resistance movement would also permanently extinguish the conditions that produce such movements is not a strategic argument. It is a wish.

If Hezbollah were to be fully dismantled tomorrow - its weapons seized, its leadership killed, scattered, its infrastructure leveled - the occupation of southern Lebanon would not thereby become more acceptable to those living under it.

The displacement of hundreds of thousands of Lebanese civilians, the destruction of villages, the cutting off of the south from the rest of the country by the systematic demolition of bridges over the Litani: these are not conditions that produce reconciliation. They are conditions that produce the next generation of resistance.

History does not repeat itself mechanically, but it does offer patterns. Resistance movements are not simply organizations. They are responses to conditions. Eliminate the organization while sustaining the conditions, and you have not solved the problem. You have restarted the clock.

X

The Precedent That Is Being Deliberately Ignored

Before examining what a durable settlement requires, it is worth examining what the historical record already tells us about how Lebanon's conflicts have actually been resolved - because the answer is more instructive than policymakers care to admit.

The 2006 war between Israel and Hezbollah lasted 34 days and ended not with a military victor, but through UN Security Council Resolution 1701, adopted unanimously on August 11, 2006.

The resolution called for an immediate ceasefire, the withdrawal of Israeli forces, the deployment of Lebanese troops and an expanded UNIFIL peacekeeping force of up to 15,000 to southern Lebanon, and the establishment of a buffer zone between the Litani River and the Blue Line - free of unauthorized armed personnel.

The Blue Line itself is not a formal international border but a 120-kilometer temporary line of withdrawal set by the UN in 2000, originally drawn to confirm the Israeli departure from southern Lebanon following the eighteen-year occupation. It has served as the reference point for every subsequent negotiation - and every subsequent violation.

Crucially, all negotiations were formally conducted with the Lebanese government. Hezbollah was never a party at the table in name. But it was always a party in fact. This pattern - formal engagement with Beirut, de facto acknowledgment of Hezbollah's role - is not a diplomatic anomaly. It is the reality of Lebanese political geography, and every mediator who has succeeded has understood it.

XI

The 2022 Maritime Agreement

The most compelling proof came in October 2022, when the United States brokered the first-ever maritime border agreement between Israel and Lebanon - the only internationally recognized, permanently demarcated boundary between the two countries, which remain technically in a state of war.

The agreement was negotiated with the Lebanese government. But what drove it to completion, and what shaped its terms, was Hezbollah. The entire agreement was vetted by the organization's top leadership prior to approval, and Nasrallah personally provided security guarantees that Israel's Karish gas field would not be targeted.

Critically, before Hezbollah's intervention, some Lebanese political figures handling the file were prepared to waive significant maritime rights - accepting less than what Lebanon's own government had formally claimed. It was Hezbollah's coercive pressure that stiffened the Lebanese position and produced an outcome Lebanon's government might not have secured alone.

The method of signing reflects this reality precisely: because Israel and Lebanon have no diplomatic relations, the agreement came as separate exchanges of letters between the US and each party, with both sides signing in separate rooms at UNIFIL headquarters in Naqoura. Lebanon's president, prime minister, and parliamentary speaker endorsed it. So did Hezbollah.

This is the inconvenient truth the current military campaign refuses to reckon with: in every instance where Lebanon and Israel have reached any durable arrangement - 1701, the Blue Line, the 2022 maritime boundary - the Lebanese government served as the formal partner while resistance forces operated as an essential background reality.

The fantasy that Lebanon can be negotiated with while its most powerful internal actor is simply eliminated has no precedent in success. It has considerable precedent in failure.

The lesson is not that Hezbollah should be rewarded or legitimized unconditionally. The lesson is that durable agreements in Lebanon have always required acknowledging the political landscape as it actually exists - not as outsiders wish it were.

XII

What a Durable Settlement Would Actually Require

None of this is to argue that Hezbollah presents no genuine security challenge to northern Israel, or that Iranian influence in Lebanon is benign. These are real concerns, and any serious analysis must acknowledge them.

But security for Israeli civilians in the north and sovereignty and dignity for Lebanese civilians in the south are not inherently incompatible. They are incompatible only with a framework premised on permanent military control of Lebanese territory.

A settlement with any prospect of durability would need to address Lebanese sovereignty over its own south, the return of displaced civilians, a timeline for Israeli military withdrawal, and - crucially - the underlying political and economic grievances that have made southern Lebanon fertile ground for armed movements for fifty years.

UN Security Council Resolution 1701, which ended the 2006 war, outlined such a framework. It was never implemented.

XIII

The Reciprocity Question

Here is a question that has gone largely unasked in Western diplomatic circles: if a military-free buffer zone in southern Lebanon is a legitimate security demand - and there is a reasonable case that it is - then why has no party formally demanded the same reciprocal arrangement in northern Israel?

If the logic of demilitarization is sound, it is sound on both sides of a border. A genuine peace architecture would include mutual buffer zones: southern Lebanon free of armed non-state actors, and northern Israel's border communities equally subject to verifiable restraints on military infrastructure that threatens Lebanese territory.

This is not a radical proposition. The 1979 Egypt-Israel peace treaty included a demilitarized Sinai precisely because both parties recognized that security demands cut in both directions. The principle of reciprocity is not a concession - it is the foundation of any agreement that both sides can actually live with.

That this demand has never been seriously tabled by Lebanon's international advocates, or pressed by any mediating power, speaks to the profound asymmetry in how this conflict is being framed and managed. A peace process that asks one side to disarm while the other faces no equivalent constraint is not a peace process. It is a terms-of-surrender document dressed in diplomatic language.

The current trajectory - delayed diplomacy, expanding occupation, land declared a "security zone" with no timeline for return - does not lead toward that settlement. It leads toward a repetition of 1982: an occupation that generates the very resistance it claims to prevent, sustained until the cost becomes politically unbearable, and concluded without having resolved anything.

XIV

A Final Note on Accountability

American policy in this moment deserves direct scrutiny. The United States brokered a ceasefire with Iran and then extended latitude to its closest ally to conduct an escalating military campaign in Lebanon that Iran has explicitly named as a dealbreaker.

The administration has told Israel it supports "freedom of action against threats on all fronts, including Lebanon," even as that freedom of action directly undermines the diplomatic framework the US itself constructed.

These are not passive contradictions. They are choices. And they will have consequences - not only for Lebanon and Iran, but for whatever post-conflict order the United States eventually hopes to claim credit for brokering.

The hard question - what comes after Hezbollah, if it is ever truly dismantled - will not wait for a convenient moment to be answered. It is being answered now, on the ground, by the conditions being created. Those who will live with the answer are already watching.

Sources and Further Reading

This essay draws on reporting, documentation, and analysis from the following sources:

  1. Al Jazeera - reporting on Lebanon, Israel, Iran, ceasefire diplomacy, and regional military escalation.
  2. NBC News - reporting on US policy, regional security concerns, and American public opinion surrounding military action in the Middle East.
  3. CNN - reporting on US foreign policy, Israeli military operations, Iran-related developments, and regional diplomatic negotiations.
  4. The Times of Israel - reporting on Israeli government statements, military policy, and debates over Lebanon and the northern border.
  5. Encyclopaedia Britannica - background documentation on the 2026 Iran war and related regional developments.
  6. Rolling Stone MENA - analysis of the Litani River's historical and strategic significance.
  7. United Nations Security Council Resolution 1701 - the 2006 framework for ceasefire, Israeli withdrawal, Lebanese army deployment, UNIFIL expansion, and the buffer zone between the Litani River and the Blue Line.
  8. United Nations Blue Line documentation - background on the temporary line of withdrawal established in 2000 after Israel's departure from southern Lebanon.
  9. Public records and reporting on the 2022 Israel-Lebanon maritime border agreement, including the US-brokered exchange of letters and the role of Lebanese state institutions.

title: I Didn't Set Out to Start Echoes of Gaza. I Just Couldn't Look Away.
subtitle:
I always thought that to do something like this, you had to be an activist.
Or someone with enough political knowledge.
shareDescription:
I always thought that to do something like this, you had to be an activist.
Or someone with enough political knowledge.

author.name: Musarrat
author.bio: Organizer of Echoes of Gaza and Solopreneur

content:
I always thought that to do something like this, you had to be an activist.
Or someone with enough political knowledge.

Someone who understood history, international law, diplomacy, organising, movements, strategy - all the things I felt I did not know enough about.
Because truth be told, I am your next-door person.
I was not the most political person in the room. I used to think politics was too complicated, too heavy, too messy, and that maybe I did not need to get my head into it.
But what I did not realise then was that everything is political.
And more than that, me saying, I don't want to meddle with politics, came from a place of privilege.
Because some people do not get the choice to look away.
Some people are born into the consequences of politics. Some people inherit the wounds of borders, occupation, famine, displacement, and war.
As an immigrant from Bangladesh residing in Singapore, my bloodline has witnessed and lived through genocide and famine.
And even if I was not connected to Palestinians through this shared history of trauma, 7 October 2023 still felt like an alarm bell ringing through my whole being.
It was the moment something in me said:
Enough is enough.
And I know I was not the only one.
I think that wake-up call shook thousands, maybe millions, of people awake around the world.
I am just a human from a dot on the map
Singapore is small.
A dot on the map.
But it is also a powerful dot in Southeast Asia.
I understand that Singapore has always had to be diplomatic to survive. I understand the importance of maintaining relationships, being strategic, and not making reckless political moves.
But we were also once occupied.
We know what it means to live under colonial violence. We know what it means for ordinary people to be at the mercy of powerful states. We know what it means when history is written by those with more weapons, more influence, and more control over the narrative.
So to remain "neutral" while Palestinians were being wiped out - not just in one moment, but over decades - felt deeply hypocritical to me.
It felt like we were choosing comfort over conscience.
And that realisation was uncomfortable.
Because once you see it, you cannot unsee it.
I didn't know what to do with the grief
In those early days, I did not know what to do with the amount of grief and helplessness I was carrying.
It felt like too much to hold in my body.
I would scroll through updates from Gaza and feel my heart break over and over again.
Families killed.
Children buried.
Journalists targeted.
Hospitals bombed.
Homes flattened.
Names becoming numbers.
And yet, life around me continued as usual.
People went to work.
People posted like nothing was happening.
People talked about weekend plans.
People moved on.
But I could not.
So I started talking to my friends about it.
One of them mentioned the White Rose movement, and that conversation stayed with me.
The White Rose was a non-violent resistance group in Nazi Germany, started by ordinary students who used leaflets, writing, and public messaging to speak against the atrocities happening around them.
And I think that was what struck me the most.
They were not powerful people. They were not politicians. They were not an army. They did not have institutions behind them.
They were young people who understood that silence, in the face of state violence, was not neutral.
They used what they had.
Words.
Paper.
Conviction.
Moral courage.
And there was something about that which felt both terrifying and deeply grounding to me.
Because sometimes we think resistance has to look grand. We think it has to be loud, perfectly organised, or led by people who know exactly what they are doing.
But the White Rose reminded me that resistance can begin with something as simple as a written message.
A leaflet.
A letter.
A sentence that refuses to be silent.
That conversation became the seed of Echoes of Gaza's first initiative:
#CeasefireLetters.

The idea was simple.
We would write letters to our Prime Minister calling for a ceasefire.
It was not a big organisation.
It was not a polished campaign.
It was not something backed by institutions or funding.
It was just a group of ordinary people trying to do something with our grief.

And I will admit, I went a little dramatic with it.
I painted the envelopes with red handprints - a visual metaphor to say that our leaders, too, have blood on their hands when they choose silence, neutrality, or inaction in the face of mass killing.
Looking back, it was bold.
But at that time, every act of resistance felt scary.
Why was writing a letter scary?
My parents eventually saw me doing it.
They saw the letters.
They saw the red handprints.
They saw me putting this initiative together.
And they got worried.
They told me not to do it.
Not because they did not care about Palestine. Not because they disagreed with the cause. But because they were afraid it might get me into trouble.
And I remember thinking:
Trouble?
For writing a letter?
To my own leaders?
That moment really stayed with me.
Because it made me realise how deeply fear lives in us. How even a simple act of civic participation - writing to the people who are meant to represent us - can feel dangerous.

And then another thought hit me:
Do I really have that much right as a citizen if I am afraid to speak to my own leaders?
Because on the surface, we are told that we live in a democracy. But deep down, many of us know how much fear shapes public expression in Singapore.
We are careful with what we say.
We are careful with where we say it.
We are careful with how much we associate ourselves with causes that are seen as "sensitive."
And it made me realise how democracy can exist on paper while fear still lives in people's bodies.
That fear is not imagined.
It is inherited.
It is learned.
It is reinforced.
And when it comes to Palestine, many Singaporeans care deeply, but they do not always know what they are allowed to do with that care.
Our first event was not just an event. It was a release.
Soon after the #CeasefireLetters campaign, we held our first virtual event in November 2023.

It was an art and cultural event.
We shared poetry.
We shared reflections.
We shared art.
We shared music.
We shared how we were feeling about the genocide.

And the space was so needed.
Everyone who joined was emotional. Not in a performative way, but in the way people become emotional when they finally realise they are not grieving alone.
It was collective grief.
And there was something strange and painful about that.
Because why did we even need to create a space to grieve one of the biggest tragedies of our lifetime?
Why did it feel like we did not even have the freedom to grieve openly?
Why did grief itself feel political?
That event made us realise something important:
People needed space.
Not just to learn.
Not just to organise.
Not just to act.
But to feel.
To process.
To witness.
To remember.
To be with other people who were also trying to make sense of the unbearable.
That was when Echoes of Gaza began to take shape

After that first event, something became clear.
We could continue holding space.
We could continue echoing the Palestinian story.
Not just the story of war.
Not just the story of death.
Not just the story of destruction.
But the story of Palestinians as people.
Their art.
Their culture.
Their poetry.
Their food.
Their music.
Their land.
Their memory.
Their humour.
Their resistance.
Their ordinary, beautiful, human lives.
Because one of the most dangerous things about oppression is how it makes the oppressed seem distant.
Foreign.
Complicated.
Too far away.
Too different from us.
And I think part of our work is to make sure Palestinians are not seen as strangers.
To bring their stories closer.
To make people understand that Palestine is not just a political issue somewhere else.
It is a human story.
A cultural story.
A moral story.
A story about land, memory, survival, and the right to live freely.
Most of us were never trained to resist
Most Singaporeans live within our own bubble.
We have our own local issues, our own pressures, our own systems to navigate. And because of the way we grew up, many of us were not trained to look at global politics in a way that asks us to act.
Especially not in a way that involves protest, public pressure, organising, or sustained solidarity.
So the muscle to do something about injustice elsewhere is often absent.
Not because people do not care.
But because many of us were never taught how to turn care into action.
We were taught to be practical.
To be safe.
To not rock the boat.
To trust institutions.
To keep our heads down.
To focus on our own lives.
But Palestine disrupted that.
Palestine reminded many of us that neutrality is not always neutral.
Sometimes neutrality protects the oppressor.
Sometimes silence becomes complicity.
Sometimes looking away is also a political choice.
And slowly, many pro-Palestinian communities in Singapore began to grow.
People began practising the muscle of resistance.
The muscle of speaking up.
The muscle of showing up.
The muscle of refusing to forget.
The muscle of asking, "What can I do from where I am?"
Echoes of Gaza was born from ordinary people choosing not to look away
I did not set out to create an organisation.
I did not sit down with a five-year plan.
I did not know what Echoes of Gaza would become.
It began with grief.
It began with helplessness.
It began with anger.
It began with a few people saying, "We have to do something."
And maybe that is how many movements begin.
Not with perfect clarity.
But with refusal.
A refusal to be numb.
A refusal to stay silent.
A refusal to let Palestinians be reduced to numbers.
A refusal to let Gaza become background noise.
Over time, Echoes of Gaza became more than a campaign or a page.
It became a space where people could gather, learn, grieve, remember, and act.
It became a space for people who were tired of feeling helpless but did not know where to begin.
It became a reminder that ordinary people are not powerless.
We may not always have institutional power.
We may not always have perfect answers.
We may not always know the exact next step.
But we have our voices.
We have our communities.
We have our skills.
We have our grief.
We have our love.
We have our refusal.
And no action is too small when it is done in service of truth.
A letter matters.
A post matters.
A conversation matters.
A gathering matters.
A resource matters.
A person choosing not to look away matters.
The work has always been about memory
When I think about Echoes of Gaza now, I think about memory.
Because so much of what is happening to Palestinians is not only physical destruction. It is also erasure.
Land is stolen.
Archives are destroyed.
Stories are distorted.
History is rewritten.
Names disappear.
Evidence gets buried.
People begin to question what they once witnessed in real time.
That is why we continue to document.
That is why we continue to gather stories.
That is why we continue to create spaces of remembrance.
Because preserving truth is also part of resistance.
And maybe that is what Echoes of Gaza has always been trying to do.
To echo what the world tries to silence.
To remember what power wants us to forget.
To keep saying:
Palestinians were here.
Palestinians are here.
Palestinians have names, homes, dreams, histories, culture, and futures.

Collaboration has carried this work
One of the most beautiful and unexpected parts of this journey has been the people it has brought into my life.
People I may never have crossed paths with otherwise.
Our worlds collided because of Gaza.
Not because we planned it.
Not because we came from the same place.
Not because we had the same background or the same entry point into this work.
But because we were all moved by the same truth:
What is happening to Palestinians is unbearable.
And we cannot look away.
That is what collaboration and solidarity have taught me.
None of this work is meant to be carried alone.
Movements are not built by one heroic person doing everything.
They are built by many ordinary people choosing to do something, again and again.
They are built in shared documents.
In late-night messages.
In dry runs.
In feedback.
In small ideas that become campaigns.
In strangers becoming collaborators.
In grief becoming action.
I still do not have all the answers
I am still learning.
I am still unlearning.
I still do not know everything about politics, history, organising, or resistance.
But I no longer believe that not knowing enough is a reason to do nothing.
Because if we wait until we feel ready, we may never begin.
And if we wait until we know everything, we risk staying silent while people are being erased.
Echoes of Gaza began because I could not look away.
But it has continued because so many others chose not to look away either.
And that gives me hope.
Not the soft, passive kind of hope.
But the kind of hope that is built through action.
The kind that says:
We may not be able to stop everything today.
But we can refuse to participate in forgetting.
We can document.
We can educate.
We can gather.
We can speak.
We can pressure.
We can remember.
We can keep the truth alive.
And sometimes, that is where a movement begins.
With one person who cannot look away.
And then another.
And then another.
Until the echo becomes impossible to ignore.

title: Incident at Beth Jacob: My Response
subtitle: I have had over a month to mull this situation over in my head. I have been dealing with a lot of complicated thoughts and emotions when it comes to this topic, but I feel that if I'm going to experience any kind of closure from this, I need to say something.
shareDescription: I have been dealing with a lot of complicated thoughts and emotions when it comes to this topic, but I feel that if I'm going to experience any kind of closure from this, I need to say something.

author.name: Alexandria Gary King / נחמה בת אברהם ושרה

content:
Note: I will not be discussing anything outside of the scope of what I have witnessed firsthand. I know that this topic has had some impact outside of what I have been able to see personally, but I have no intention of speculating on events that are outside of my purview.

I have had over a month to mull this situation over in my head. I have been dealing with a lot of complicated thoughts and emotions when it comes to this topic, but I feel that if I'm going to experience any kind of closure from this, I need to say something. This is not a piece defending myself or an attempt to attack anyone. If it were up to me, this never would have happened, and I would have continued as things were, but unfortunately that is impossible. I'm sure that many people reading this have no idea what I'm talking about, so I'll lay down some background, explain what happened, and attempt to clarify some points. Through this, I hope to lay a better path forward not only for myself, but also hopefully for the greater Jewish community.

First, for those who don't know who I am, I am Alexandria King (Hebrew Name: נחמה בת אברהם ושרה) and I am a twenty-nine-year-old college student in Dayton, Ohio, direct support professional, and a proud Jew by choice. I am a member of Temple Israel (a reform synagogue in Dayton) and had been attending services at Beth Jacob Synagogue (an Orthodox synagogue) off and on for some time. The appeal of attending Beth Jacob services despite the fact that I am not the most frum (observant) person was that I found great comfort in the ritual of the Orthodox service. I enjoyed coming in early, going through the liturgy, and hearing the entire Torah and Haftorah portions. Most importantly, I always looked forward to Rabbi Agar's drashes (sermons). I was always interested to see where he was going to take the portion and what stories he was going to tell; however, services didn't always go smoothly. I can recall on at least one occasion where service was interrupted by someone going into the hallway and throwing a fit (this is what those in the storytelling business call foreshadowing.)

It was the 28th of February when, as was normal for me at this point, I went to services at Beth Jacob. Nothing was out of the ordinary, me and my partner Isaac were worshiping on separate sides of the מחיצה (mechitza, a divider at an Orthodox Synagogue to split men and women) when suddenly there was a commotion in the hallway. There was a woman who was terribly upset about someone being allowed at the synagogue. It was hard to make it out, but they were yelling phrases such as "sin against god," "they're not a member," "anti-Israel," "pro-Pali," and "it's them or me." There was a point in that diatribe in which I was pretty certain that this person was referring to me, and when I looked back to see Leibel (Rabbi Agar) gesturing me to come back, my suspicions were confirmed. He asked me to leave. I was willing, albeit with consternation, quietly and without fuss. That is until the woman, as I was leaving, decided that she wanted to put salt on the wound and say things to my face. I was already in fight or flight mode, a feeling that was remarkably familiar to me and was reminiscent of times I have been physically attacked, and decided to tell her, in some impolite words, to get out of my face. From there I left and began to have an emotional breakdown in my car. The next week, I was contacted by a detective from the Sheriff's office that I was asked to not return to the synagogue because of MY disturbance and rude language (and specifically not because of my views or where I was sitting, according to them.)

I have had a lot of feelings about this situation, but the main one is one of deep hurt. I felt like I was being abandoned by someone I once considered a friend and a trusted community leader. I felt like I was no longer safe in my Jewish Community because of who I am. A woman who I have never met before comes in and yells about me, and I'm the one who must leave? It makes no sense to me, and it feels like they were looking for an excuse not to have me come, and this just fell into their laps. I can't honestly think of any other explanation than either malice or incompetence, and I'd believe either one at this point.

Now, I want to make one thing explicit: yes, I am and have always been staunchly for the self-determination of the Palestinian people. I believe that Israel's actions on that front have been and continue to be abhorrent. At the same time, I believe deeply in the safety, dignity, and self-determination of Jewish people. I do not at all believe that these commitments are contradictory. I am a person who believes that if we want something to be better, we must be able to criticize it. The Torah calls for us to be a holy nation and to pursue justice justly, and I do not believe that the state of Israel holds itself to that standard. I will continue to work toward betterment; however, that does not make me an antisemite or worthy of being excluded from Jewish spaces.

To conclude, I just want to say that I am not sure what will come out of this. I know that there have already been discussions about what happened on that day to which I am not privy. Despite my efforts to keep this in my small circle of disclosure, I should've known that it was going to get out regardless of who I spoke to. I just want to say that even though I am hurt still, this is not something that happens in all Jewish Spaces. There have been countless spaces that my Queer Pro-Palestinian Jewish self has felt wholly welcome in. This is more so a cautionary tale that not all Jewish spaces are safe, and that not all leadership can lead.
